verses.text: Solomon allies with Egypt by marrying Pharaoh's daughter, and though he loves the LORD, he and the people still sacrifice at the high places. A real love for God can sit beside compromises not yet surrendered. Even sincere faith has corners it has not let God fully cleanse.

Solomon allies with Egypt by marrying Pharaoh's daughter, and though he loves the LORD, he and the people still sacrifice at the high places. A real love for God can sit beside compromises not yet surrendered. Even sincere faith has corners it has not let God fully cleanse.

Solomon allies with Egypt by marrying Pharaoh's daughter, and though he loves the LORD, he and the people still sacrifice at the high places. A real love for God can sit beside compromises not yet surrendered. Even sincere faith has corners it has not let God fully cleanse.

At Gibeon Solomon offers a thousand burnt offerings, and God appears in a dream: 'Ask what I shall give thee.' The young king who seeks God lavishly is met with an open invitation. God delights to meet the heart that comes to Him generously and early.

At Gibeon Solomon offers a thousand burnt offerings, and God appears in a dream: 'Ask what I shall give thee.' The young king who seeks God lavishly is met with an open invitation. God delights to meet the heart that comes to Him generously and early.

Solomon asks not for riches or long life or victory, but for 'an understanding heart to judge thy people,' confessing he is 'but a little child.' He feels the weight of his task and his own smallness. The best prayer asks God for what we need to serve Him and others well.

God is pleased and grants the wisdom asked — and adds riches and honour Solomon did not ask, with long life if he obeys. Seek first the things of God, and the rest is often added. He who asks for the right thing receives both it and more from a generous God.

Solomon awakes, returns to Jerusalem, stands before the ark, and offers sacrifices and a feast. The dream drives him to worship and to remember God's covenant. A true encounter with God does not leave us where it found us; it sends us back to His altar in thanks.

Two harlots come before the king, each claiming the one living child after the other's baby died in the night. There is no witness, only two women and contradicting words. The hardest cases bring no easy proof — and it is just there that God-given wisdom is needed.

Two harlots come before the king, each claiming the one living child after the other's baby died in the night. There is no witness, only two women and contradicting words. The hardest cases bring no easy proof — and it is just there that God-given wisdom is needed.

Each insists the living child is hers and the dead one the other's, their voices clashing before the king with no way to tell true from false. Bare assertion meets bare assertion. Where everyone claims to be in the right, only deeper discernment can find the truth.

Solomon calls for a sword: divide the living child in two, half to each. The shocking command is a test, not a sentence. Wisdom sometimes presses hard on a situation, knowing that what is truly in the heart will reveal itself under pressure.

At once the true mother cries, give her the child, only do not kill it; the other says divide it. Real love would rather lose the child than see it die. Solomon gives the baby to the mother whose heart was revealed, and all Israel sees the wisdom of God is in him.
